CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Meeting with Toni Brownfield reviewing the Swept BAF application. Toni requested inclusion of customer discovery, clearer language for reviewers that are not up to speed with the latest AI terminology, and also extracting the vendor explanation language to a separate supporting document with key aspects for special expertise and prior successful history. I rewrote a draft from the existing language with that emphasis, and other wordsmithing to improve clarity and readability. Toni clarified some aspect for the letters of support which I relayed to the CEO. A subsequent proposal review revealed anomalies in the Financial Forecast for EBITDA trend, ASP trend, and S&M drop that will be explained/addressed.

From: Brent D. Gettel

Sent: Thursday, May 29, 2025 4:23 PM

To: William Surles <willsurles@gmail.com>

Subject: Follow-up to Recent Meeting

 

William-

Thank you again for meeting with me to go over the ventures you're considering for a small business. Following and attached are some items related to our discussion:

Ted faced several challenges in preparing a compelling investor pitch for Clean Energy, his clean label sports and energy drink startup. While he had a strong foundation with $110,000 in early sales, clear market differentiation, and partnerships with athletic departments, the messaging and presentation needed refinement to better highlight those strengths. He needed to simplify technical language, clarify his clean label claim, and communicate the brand’s potential in a way that resonated with investors. Ted also had to improve visual impact, structure the financial story around achievable milestones like $10M in sales, and strike a persuasive tone in both slides and delivery without relying on subjective or defensive claims.


I helped Ted restructure the presentation to be visually clean and focused on investor-relevant takeaways. We simplified the language, adjusted slide formatting for stronger visual hierarchy, and replaced unclear or debatable claims with credi

Summary: The client tried to submit the pitch but she thinks to submit anything, she needs to add a new role. However when she tried to add herself as PI, it didn't accept her UEI from sam.gov. She sent a screenshot and said she needs help.


Action: After looking over her information, I responded that she does not need to access research.gov to submit their project pitch. Nor do they need to add a role at this time. That is only for the full proposal. To submit the pitch, go to https://nsfiip.force.com/mywork/s/login/?ec=302&startURL=%2Fmywork%2Fs%2F. They will have to create an account here, but it doesn’t require anything but their name and email address.


Plan: She will let me know if they have any problems and we can walk through it.

A FOLLOW UP MESSAGE FROM RITA HILLMAN

Rita Hillman <

Summary/Action: I reached out to the client and said it was great talking to them last week. I sent the information I promised about setting up/talking to TPOCs/Technical Authors/Program Managers. Note that this can only be done privately until 5/28. It is of the upmost importance that they (try to) talk to the TPOC(s) BEFORE the solicitation officially opens. This will accomplish two things – It will introduce they to them (they are their primary (and maybe only) reviewer so this is important) so they recognize they when they see their document and it will also answer any questions that they may have regarding the topic announcement. THIS IS NOT INTENDED TO BE A SALES PITCH. They are not trying to sell their idea to them; they are trying to glean any information that will help they write and target their proposal to fit their needs. Here are the recommendations on how the contact with the TPOC should go: I recommend the initial request should be done via email ASAP to make an appoi
Client was a no show for the meeting today due to being ill. I've emailed him to let me know when he would like to reschedule.

Business Plan Templates From Small Business Development Center (SBDC)

Hello DaNetta,


Happy Friday! I'm so glad you visited us at the Small Business Support HUB yesterday afternoon. I sent out an email yesterday evening regarding the 90 Day Business Refinement Cohort starting on June 2nd, but I also wanted to send you the business plan templates I promised. They are attached to this email.


They are in PDF format, but open up great in either Word or Google Docs. Let me know if you'd like a different format.


I've included my calendar link as well, so you can find a time that works best

I spoke to Ms. Jayla Dallas concerning her business idea. Ms. Dallas is interested in creating a commercial and residential cleaning company. Currently, Ms. Dallas has two clients which is her strategy to determine how long it takes to provide the service she will offer as well as whether or not she would like cleaning properties. Ms. Dallas indicated that this business start up is something she would like to pursue and need assistance in developing a business plan. She has collect some information that can be used in a plan however, she had no template to use currently. I asked if she downloaded the Score's Business Plan and she indicated she did not download the plan. I sent a copy of the plan for her review. We discuss certain segments of the business plan such as marketing, financials, insurance and the importance of a strong labor strategy. I suggested that she research her competition to see what services they provided, their focus, how long in business and pricing. I also

ClientIntake.CompanyName: Compassion by Heart Home Care Services LLC

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I reviewed Davina's business plan and gave her feedback. I told her it was confusing, with too many programs and ideas, and that it was hard to sort out what was happening in which order.


We also discussed the accounting / financial management training and how she needs to understand the timing and amount of her costs. I urged her to calculate her start-up costs, subtract what she can for the LRL grant, and then determine how much more she'll need to get started.


We also discussed calculating her desired patient mix, separating private pay patients from Medicaid, so that she understands how she can cover her costs and make money.


She is also interested in potentially operating as a nonprofit, which may make sense but will add complications.
CRITICAL ISSUE: Approval of work order for cohort funds. Email from Stacy at EDA with said order, reviewed, and it was exactly what we discussed and lines up with her milestones. Approved.
Meeting about Saginaw Soup prep for presentation

Outreach after no contact

From: Brent D. Gettel

Sent: Monday, June 9, 2025 11:13 AM

To: justinfreeland26@gmail.com <justinfreeland26@gmail.com>

Subject: Michigan SBDC check-in

 

Hi Justin - Just doing a follow-up from the SBDC to see if there is anything you would like to discuss regarding your business ventures you were considering. If so, please let me know if you have any questions or would like to set up a meeting through my booking link at the bottom of my email signature. 

If you have decided to move on to something else or no longer need the SBDC services, just reply to this email and I can move your account from active to a holding status. In the future, if you need further assistance we can always reopen the file.

 

Sincerely,

I called our client to see if she was still interested in pursuing her project. I left a voicemail for her to call me back. Since our initial contact she has not been responsive to phone calls or email.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Harry and I met with Christie to form her LLC and get her EIN. We walked her through the process and made sure she was comfortable with everything involved in owning an LLC. She picked the name Cook’s Adult Foster Care, LLC. After forming the LLC, Christie mentioned only needing a few more things before being able to proceed with the loan. She needs the updated balance sheet and projections, which Harry and I will complete. We also asked Christie to get an insurance quote to include in her projections.

Meeting Resources. Research. Sent email


It was a pleasure meeting with you and discussing your business. Here are the resources I mentioned. 


Dropshipping

https://sell.amazon.com/learn/what-is-dropshipping

https://www.dsers.com/blog/international-dropshipping/

https://www.fedex.com/en-us/small-business/articles-insights/what-is-drop-shipping.html


Business Plan: https://www.score.org/sites/default/files/2023-02/SCORE-Startup-Business-Plan-Template.docx


Cash Flow By Month: https://michigansbdc.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/Cash-Flow-Projection-and-Variance-Analysis-Spreadsheet.xlsx

They also discussed the cost structure of their product, potential partnerships, and upcoming family cruises. Lastly, they discussed Avery's pricing model and a potential Facebook pilot, with Nancy offering to introduce Avery to another founder.

Next steps

Avery to send Nancy the completed pricing model for review.

Nancy to forward Avery's marketing document to the home care company founder and ask permission to connect them.

Hi Avril,


Sorry for the delay getting back. I was on the road the other day. Thanks for forwarding your business plan to me.


As we discussed, any bank is going to want three things:

1. A credit score of at the very least 600. 700 and above is the most ideal

2. Some kind of collateral

3. That you have a business plan that shows financial projections, usually 2 years into the future. They especially want you to show that you will have enough cash to make your loan payment, and to cover your rent and expenses. 


As I also mentioned, the amount you say you need, $5000, is not much. You should also consider other ways to get this amount to fund the business. 


I have attached the template for a simple business plan. This is the one the SBDC uses, and banks are familiar with. I would take the plan you

Zoom session with Janis Seabolt (AA Spark) to discuss Strut Motors. AAS provided $15k funding for marketing, web site development and launch support. Besides web site, not much has been done so far. Compared notes on separate recent sessions with the founder, Ilan Penn. We question fundamental product logic -- an urban EV capped to 25mph, but loaded with ADAS tech and some form of cargo / passenger modularity. It sounds similar in concept to the recently announced Bezos-backed SLATE pickup / utility EV, but the SLATE can hit highway speeds. Also wondered why recent pilot grant and prototype grant applications were rejected, and whether Ilan had asked for detailed feedback and advice from OFME / Centrepolis. Janis has a call scheduled with Ilan next week. I have been working calendars to get him on a session with Bob Honeyman for a first round financial modeling session.

PROBLEM

Strategic planning, resource mapping, financial planning


DISCUSSION

  • She is not doing well financially.

  • She’s also babysitting for her partners grandson at the business as needed, there has been no revenue generation. Startup costs have been 65-70K and has it all financed, mostly has been equipment. Worked in college athletics for 15 years and started with her own brand- she is former college athlete herself

Prob- she’s on borrowed time to make money, building per month $1600 (including utilities). In 2024 she made $1200 – A2 BandI networking group and has a coach now who’s helping her.

Worked on business plan for Michelle in LivePlan.

Building in the Soo - we talked about Tammy's Spiritual Leadership business today. Tammy doesn't want to get too big too fast but she isn't doing any marketing so she has no clients. However in the same breath Tammy is hooked on the idea of having a building in Sault Ste. Marie that is only $600 a month. Together we figured out how much she would need to make in a month to be able to afford the building.


Rent $600

Internet $100

building insurance $200

Business insurance $25

Utilities $100 (total guess)


Tammy wants to bring home $2,500 a month before taxes ($500 a month would be put away for personal taxes)

Gas would run $208 a month ( three trips a week at four weeks a month)


Tammy charges $60 per hour So $3,733 divided by $60 = 62 clients a month


I encouraged Tammy to start working to build her Facebook following so she has a base of followers when she's ready to open

The meeting involved Nancy Benovich Gilby, Tracy, leading a tech startup, likely referred to as "Founder" below), and Janet Wyllie. The main discussion centered on the Founder's current project with "the Loons," which involves a new technology that is seeing high user interest but is hampered by poor placeholder content and significant "fetching" costs on their Bubble platform.

A critical upcoming task is creating a promotional video by a hard deadline of June 14th. This video is essential for Jeffrey Pollock, who intends to use it to secure a potentially transformative deal with ASM Global.

Funding for projects like the video and a "data caching" solution (to reduce fetching costs) was a key topic. Shelby shared updates with Nancy. The good news is she finally received the board game contract from Ferris State University. The main challenge revolves around funding and IP for the software version. Ferris will match grants but not directly invest, and the Advanced Grant requires a Michigan-based professional software vendor. Due to Ferris cutting ties with "Ydie" (a previous software option), Shelby, with guidance from Janet at Spartan Innovations, plans to use $10,000-$15,000 from various funds Janet has access to for an MVP. This route would also mean Kelsh Health retains 100% IP, a point Ferris verbally agreed to, but Shelby needs this in writing.

Hello,

Hi Marc,

It was great meeting you! I really enjoyed hearing about your plans to start your own window and pressure washing business, especially with the experience you’ve gained over the past 20 years, starting with your grandfather’s company.

Here’s a quick summary of what we discussed:

  • You’re planning to start the business part-time on weekends while continuing to work your full-time job. Your goal is to earn an additional $10,000–$20,000 per year.
  • You currently have the equipment you need and are considering upgrading your truck in the future.
  • Your target customers are older adults and middle-aged individuals who don’t have the time or ability to wash windows themselves.
  • You’re working on creating a business plan and are aiming to launch within the next two months (we’ll continue to evaluate if that’s a realistic timeline).
  • You’d like support with forming an LLC, getting the appropriate business license and insurance, and fig

Summary: The client wrote that she just submitted the pitch and it went well. She has a question about sam.gov. She got the UNIQUE ENTITY ID and after that she didn't do anything. She thought getting the UEI was approval. She wanted to know if that is correct or does she need to do more.


Action: I responded that it is great that she got the pitch submitted. My fingers are crossed. The UEI is not the end of the process. To check status, go to sam.gov and click on “check entity status” in the lower right corner (screenshot attached). That should tell them where they are in the process. If it tells them that they haven’t finished applying for the full registration, they will need to follow the instructions at https://www.fsd.gov/gsafsd_sp?id=kb_article_view&sysparm_article=KB0038643. I believe that I recall that they have already done this, but just in case, I am sending the full information to go through. They want to make sure that they selected “all awards”

On Mon, Jun 2, 2025 at 10:38 AM Dan Lanigan <vanderllc@outlook.com> wrote:

HI Jason, 


The struggle to launch a business is real. Most entrepreneurs are self funded in the beginning. Especially, if you are in the developmental phase and have no proof of concept. My advice is to start documenting the financials, even if it;s just your best guess.If you don't write things down on a spreadsheet then you have no way to revise or correct your numbers.  

Start by documenting Start up Costs then move on to Operating Costs, the expenses you will have to "keep the doors" open on a monthly basis. I don't expect these numbers to be perfect or this attempt to be the only time you'll work with the numbers. I have attached some different spreadsheets, please try to put realistic numbers in. This will help with the big picture of what kind of revenue you will need to breakeven.

When you get the majority of the numbers together, send them to me along with some dates with your availability.


Diana

Video call with Marc. Calendly</

Thank you for your responses. Please use https://calendly.com/southeastmisbdc/phone-call to set a meeting. 


What to Expect

  • We are funded by the Small Business Administration and our services are at no-cost (except some trainings have a small registration fee). 
  • All information we discuss will remain confidential. This information was in the initial documentation that you completed when you requested consulting.
  • Meetings usually last less than 1 hour. Meetings are only by phone and by appointment.
  • We assist both start-up and existing businesses. Some of the topics that we help with are startup steps, business plans, marketing strategies, financial benchmarking, projections and preparing for bank loans.
  • Michigan SBDC does not have any direct funding for businesses.

Hi Sheena,


Just checking in for an update on your progress. Have you continued with the Ice Cream truck business and purchased a vehicle? Please send me an update or we can schedule a follow up call to discuss any issues you are having. 


Diana

Client has modified her logo, messaging and labeling after speaking with Dana from Spout. (cannot make health claims on her microgreens). She has indeed been selling in Horrocks in Battle Creek and looking to expand to their Lansing location. (32 salads/week). Also selling at the UpRoot coop in Battle Creek. She is now at capacity for production until she can find another location. I'm connecting her with John Hart with the City of Battle Creek who can guide her to the right location. Now that she has production under control, she will be working on her SOP's then the employee handbook and job descriptions. She'll need to hire people as well to increase production. She is aware of the BCFR/SBSH grant and I'll be speaking with Gaby and Dana this week about her application. I shared the details that it is a competitive and REIMBURSABLE grant so that she understands how it will work. Next meeting will cover the review of her SOP's.

Zoom call with JD. He is getting ready to launch a newsletter through LinkedIn to support both bringing on new gyms as well as new client companies. He got four new clients, all startup related to health and nutrition, in the last few weeks. We discussec content for the newsletter. He also is looking to obtain BAF funding for Q Line media to help with the newsletter. He does not currently work with a specific SmartZone, and can relocate. We discussed various SmartZones and areas he should take into consideration when determining which one he would like to work with as well as the BAF process. He also discussed challenges he is having in Michigan is obtaining early stage funding and his application to the Conquer Accelerator.

Reworked financials and started reviewing narrative. Needs to show more revenue or decrease expenses to make idea work. He will do a customer discovery exercise and survey, and then reach out to schedule another meeting.

I met with our client, Terri Steele, by video conference. She spoke of what she had done thus far and what lies ahead in opening her mental health therapy practice. She went through an attorney to set up her LLC and obtain her EIN. She is researching various Electronic Health Records software. EHR software will determine how much she will be able to accomplish without adding more employees or hiring out bookkeeping. I am sharing the Basic Bookkeeping 2.0 template with her as I believe it shouldn't be too difficult to track expenses. We discussed that her start up costs would be relatively low and her operating costs less than $500 per month (phone, internet, insurance, accounting, EHR and so forth). Depending on her reimbursement rates from insurance companies it might only take two or three sessions per month to break even. Christie is working through the complex challenge of aligning her SaaS financial model with industry benchmarks while maintaining a realistic growth plan for investors. She’s navigating tough trade-offs between revenue growth, R&D investment, and operating income, all while planning a thoughtful rollout of Choreo's sales and marketing strategy. With a conservative sales forecast, an extended healthcare sales cycle, and a limited budget, Christie is actively balancing early design partner engagement, staffing needs, and customer acquisition timelines to build a credible, fundable business plan.


I recommended adjusting her R&D benchmarks to avoid going negative on operating income, reviewing design partner strategies from leading SaaS investors, and revisiting her sales and marketing assumptions with industry data. We discussed breaking down her $1.2M round into phases and using CRM tools to manage outreach more effectively. I also suggested revising her s

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

1:1 consultation and updates on business progress. Terrie Ahmed has worked with the SBDC in the past and reconnected with us after she attended the GEN Launch Pad open office hours at the Flint Public Library. 


Terrie is working to revamp her secondhand clothing resale business and is currently limited to using only the Poshmark platform for her online presence. She recently brought back handmade items from a trip to Ghana that represent authentic West African fashion, which presents an opportunity to expand her product line beyond secondhand clothing. 


Her business is facing significant challenges due to current tariffs and supply chain disruptions that are affecting her ability to source inventory. A recent purchase from a Japanese supplier she has worked with for 20 years was canceled because the increased costs made the transaction unprofitable, th

Yugo and I reviewed his SaaS company’s financial forecast, where one of the main challenges was ensuring that projections for revenue, expenses, and hiring were realistic and aligned with industry benchmarks. Yugo was particularly focused on balancing growth with operational efficiency, questioning whether a projected headcount of 68 by 2029 was too high and how best to model G&A costs while scaling. He also needed clarity around certain balance sheet entries, such as long-term debt, paid-in capital, and equity, as well as confirming the method for forecasting and allocating departmental expenses like G&A, R&D, and Sales & Marketing. Some discrepancies in the model—like a typo in the interest expense formula and misaligned benchmark calculations—further complicated things.


I walked Yugo through how to adjust assumptions in the model, including subscriber growth rates and departmental budget percentages, to see how these changes influenced total reven

Ethan is working on business plan adding revenue streams. His millage ($4 per mile outside of a 30 mile radius) in his mind the $4 will break down to $1 for fuel, $1 owners draw, $1 insurance, and $1 for insurance. I'm unsure how to put this into LivePlan so I'm meeting with Lance Wolfe to review it and get some guidance.


Needed to set up his EIN so he could get a quote on business insurance. So we filed that.

Pavan is developing a startup focused on repurposing EV batteries, and his main challenge has been presenting the complexity of the business in a compelling and clear way—both in his financial model and his pitch deck. He’s navigating a nuanced revenue model that includes both first-life and second-life battery applications, and he’s working to structure financial projections that align with investor expectations, particularly around unit economics, licensing revenue, and cost assumptions. Communicating expansion into utility-scale markets without confusing investors during early-stage pitches has also been difficult. Additionally, Pavan needs to improve how his financial slides are interpreted by potential investors, especially in the context of physical product sales and manufacturing costs.


I walked Pavan through a detailed financial planning spreadsheet and showed him how to model expenses, employee compensation, and revenue projections in a clear and modular wa

I asked to conduct the initial intake session because I had already spoken with the client's wife about his plans and thought it might be more efficient to move forward this way. CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Jamie Smith

I spoke to Mr. Deante Coleman about his business plan. Mr. Coleman put many hours in developing his plan. He is interested in creating a holistic farm and he is seeking three million dollars. My concern and I shared it with him is that it would be easier to sale his plan if he scale down the business plan strategy so that people would be able to digest the information. We discuss how he can narrow the focus of the plan by reducing his goal to a manageable size. The goal is to develop a closed-loop farm that produce organic veggies and fruit crops using renewable energy. Because of the extensive work he has put into his current business plan I suggested to Mr. Coleman that he could use the information in his current business plan and develop an action plan that shows how his strategy would generate revenue. Hi Kevin,

I don't know how much you know of our very small business so a little background might help. In 2010, I applied for (and currently have) patents for our unique fastener called DecoTies®. They consist of a flexible adhesive base at center and twist ties (all one piece). Designed primarily for quickly hanging tinsel garland and light strings. We had a license agreement with Glue Dots International back in 2010-2012, however, they were unable to manufacture in commercial quantities so the license was terminated. Other companies expressed strong interest including ShurTech Brands, on and off over the years.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Met with Kevin, who brought his business plan for review. We covered some of the basic info, but worked primarily on the cash needed, income, and expenses. CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Investor pitch coaching session round 2 - through New Enterprise Forum.

Jason is working through several challenges as he prepares to respond to an RFP for a loan document generation platform. He has deep domain expertise in legal customization for loan agreements but needs to package that expertise into a compelling and polished proposal. The complexity of the project—integrating mobile-first design, streamlined document upload, and scalable architecture—presents both an opportunity and a formatting challenge. He’s also navigating how to stand out from competitors, meet the June deadlines, and possibly leverage Spark funding for design support. Additionally, he’s exploring how to position his platform as both functional and visually appealing, while dealing with limitations in AI tools for document generation and seeking human support for design and proposal refinement.


I recommended that Jason take a structured approach by drafting a detailed outline first—headings, bullet points, and core text—before moving into design. We talked thr

Hi Stacy,

Thanks so much for sending over your flyer! I love seeing how you’re bringing your Coloring Parties idea to life, it’s such a fun and unique concept.

Here are a few thoughts to consider as you refine the design and build your brand identity:

  • Color palette: Try narrowing it down to 2–3 main colors that represent your brand’s personality. This will help create a consistent and recognizable look across your flyers, social media, and other materials.
  • Design tools: If you’re not already using it, I highly recommend Canva. It’s free, easy to use, and offers great templates that can help with layout and brand consistency.
  • Typography: Consider uncapitalizing the black text to give the flyer a cleaner, more polished look. Consistency in font style and formatting can make a big impact.
  • Layout & spacing: The bottom half of the flyer feels a bit crowd

Aging Client check in. Low potential

My Reply to Marc with suggestions:


Hi Marc,

This is a solid start, great work putting your ideas together. I wanted to point out a couple of things to consider as you continue refining the plan.

First, I was under the impression that you were planning to focus on residential window cleaning. Since you’ve now mentioned servicing commercial buildings as well, I just want to bring some awareness around that shift. Including both residential and commercial services can increase the premium on your business insurance, as commercial work tends to come with more liability and risk. Additionally, many commercial clients may require a formal service agre

Met with Julia at Mid Michigan College, along with Emily Gibson.

  • Since opening in November 2023, Julia has decided she does not want to continue operating Valhalla Axe Throwing. She does enjoy the day to day operations of running the business, and it is not busy enough for her to justify hiring a full management team.
  • I reviewed with her the 3 wants when selling, and risk if priority, with time right behind it. She initially stated $200,000 is what she hopes she could get, but I advised her half of that would be, in my opinion, the best she could get.
  • She has 2 potential parties interested in possibly purchasing, but after discussing, neither party is probably a good fit.
  • She brought up a third party - a local business owner - who she thought might be interested. I mentioned those owners would be only interested in it if they could see a return that met their risk profile, and part of that would involve what her 2024 tax return shows. She hasn'

I met with Julia alongside growth consultant Jeff Punches, to discuss her desire to sell Valhalla. While she loves the business, she is wanting to get back to a leisurely retirement. We discussed a few key factors about her sale:

  • She would like to get out of the business by the end of the year. Ideally, she would like to be completely done with operations, and would only want to finance as a last resort.
  • She is hoping for $200,000 for the business, but is considering what amount she would take if offered cash for it.
  • The operation is fairly turn-key - Julia has some reliable employees that mainly manage the business, but a new owner would have to go through franchise training.
  • There are a few business owners in the are that she would like to approach about purchasing it.

Jeff offered the following steps to take next:

  • Julia should talk to her accountant about her 2024 taxes, as she asked for an extension in filing. Her acc

**CONSULTATION**

Target locations: hospitals, gyms, universities, banks. Reminded him to account for short shelf life of fresh items. Funding: likely needs SBA microloan (Brian P.) and will register a DBA. She can get started today and grow into her business slowly while gathering funding

Her year of challenges made her ready to accept this message and an alternate plan to get started


Bettina was highly encouraged by this conversation and now feels empowered to get started on a small scale

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Met with clients at the site for their new nightclub. Clients walked me thru their facility and described what their vision was for the club. We went over whether or not to charge a cover charge, which tables could be utilized as a VIP area, what items to include on their menu, hours of operation and finally begin determining what level of business they may be able to pull. I drew up a proposed sales/wk based on an average $ /customer charge of $25 and what they might assume could be reasonable. I also suggested to clients that they look into recorded music given it would be hard to have live music every night. I told them what happened to me in my first restaurant where I played CDs as background music and then was visited by a BMI rep and had to pay a fine and then join BMI to be able to continue. I suggested that they look into satellite radio as the monthly fee covers their BMI rights. In the kitchen Jeremiah and Talked about what to include on their menu-3-4 appetizers, 4-6 sma

More discussion on priorities:

  1. Focus on the 2 airbnbs getting the plumbing, updates done as quickly so as to be able to book and generate sales this summer season.
  2. Focus on the larger vacant parcel; have dad who is logger do a timber cruise to establish potential wood value.
  3. Begin to outline a road system for the camping and build out some rough sites during the logging with a road/trail system; select cutting areas around sites and probably remaining to be clear cut. Determine if well/septic reports are available.
  4. Bar north of Newberry value of liquor license and transfer to item 2? Building appears to be needed to tear down what is the costs? Then what to do with the land after that?
  5. Johnnys Bar location in Newberry put a value on FFE, land, liquor license and the COST of salvaging the building after extensive water damage is it worth it?

Ryan,

Thanks for your time today and for sharing your experience and aspiration to acquire Rustica in Kalamazoo. 


As we talked through the details, on the lender side, they are looking for a credit score of at least 650 to even start the conversation. I know you are working on improving your current score. That's excellent.


Additionally, a lender will look for a potential owner to invest between 15% and 20% of their own cash into the deal. You may consider "friends and family" investors and possibly a crowdfunding campaign as two options to achieve a stronger cash position.

Hi Erica,

Thanks again for your patience and for taking the time to catch up with me last week! It was great to hear more about your business idea focused on goods transport and ride services — there’s definitely potential in that space, and I’m excited to support your progress.

As we discussed, I understand that things have been busy on your end, but you’re still thinking ahead, which is key. I’m glad to hear you’ll be attending the upcoming Chamber event — that’s a great place to network and get inspired.

Regarding funding, since your credit union requires two years of business activity before offering financial support, I recommend exploring SBA loans or financing through community banks or CDFIs (Community Development Financial Institutions) (I can assist if needed), which tend to be more flexible with new entrepreneurs.

In the meantime, here are a few next steps and resources that I think will help:

  • Ma

Thanks for the update! The flyer looks great. Regarding the Night Market, if building bears feels logistically tricky, offering a variety of coloring pages or mini coloring books could be a great alternative. You could even have a small "make-your-own cover" station where people personalize a blank cover and bind a few pages inside—that could feel more interactive. Another option is to charge a small fee for people to sit and color, especially if the atmosphere is welcoming and you provide quality materials. Vision boards could also be a good activity.

The key is what you already identified: emphasizing the experience. Think calming, creative, and unique. Maybe include a small sign that says something like:

“Take a mindful moment—color, create, and relax.”

You’re doing an awesome job, and I’m excited to see where this goes.

Sincerely,

Diego Sandoval

Business Consultant<

Summary/Action: The client and talked by phone. They talked to a Space Force TPOC about a D2P2 SBIR opportunity and they want them to submit. It's due June 25. We talked about specifics for the D2P2 Spacewerx program and the items that they specifically look for like a path to an end-user withing Space Force, and how that process would go forward. We talked about Phase I feasibility and how to talk about that, to ensure that they understand what work was done in terms of the requirements of the D2P2 program. We talked about the potential to match funding and what he can do to up his odds of success if possible.


Plan: He will send me a document for review if he has time before submission.

Summary: The client recently formed Coheron LLC, a Michigan-based company focused on building a more responsible foundation for how non-human intelligence makes decisions especially in high-stakes, life-and-death situations. He is preparing to move forward on two specific paths. The first is the upcoming SBIR DoD 25.3 solicitation, where he plans to submit a Phase-I proposal centered around real-time ethical overlays for autonomous defense systems. The second is the AI Safety Fund, which supports efforts to ensure AI is aligned with human values and safety in critical use cases. The system he’s been developing is called CODE. It’s designed as a real-time ethical decision layer that sits between the planner and the actuator in autonomous systems. It doesn’t just enforce fixed rules, it evaluates each action through multiple ethical lenses, including military law, public perception, and cost/risk considerations. CODE only allows an action to proceed when equilibrium is reached across

Summary: The client wrote that they've nearly completed their grant applications that are due on 06/25/25. Their only concern at present is SAM.gov registration. It's taken them longer than expected to get their bank account set up and are planning to submit on Thursday 06/05/25. They wanted to know if I have any connectivity to the SAM.gov administrators that could help them get through the process. They know they say it takes 7-10 business days which is cutting it close, so it would be great to be able to reach out to them to confirm there are no issues that may delay approval of their application.


Action: I responded that, unfortunately, there is no way to expedite this. They are pretty firm in the way they process. However, I will say that they seem to be coming in on time if not slightly quicker right now so if they can get it in now, they should (fingers crossed) be ok. Just keep an eye on the updates once they submit it.
